title: TopologicalOrder

tex_statement.html: Let $G=(J,V,E,L)$ be a CDMG. A topological order of $G$ is a total order $<$ of $J \cup V$ such that for all $v,w \in G$: \[ v \in \Pa^G(w) \; \implies \; v < w.\] Equivalently, it can be described as an indexing of the nodes $J \cup V = \{v_1,\dots,v_K\}$ where parents always precede their children.

lean_code_with_comments:
/-- `IsTopologicalOrder G r` -- the relation `r : α → α → Prop` is a
*topological order* of the CDMG `G`: restricted to the nodes
`G.J ∪ G.V` of `G`, the relation `r` is a strict total order
(irreflexive, transitive, trichotomous), and every parent precedes
its children (`v ∈ Pa^G(w) → r v w`). The behaviour of `r` on
vertices outside `G.J ∪ G.V` is left unconstrained. Mirrors
`lecture-notes/lecture_notes/graphs.tex` def 3.8 verbatim. -/
structure IsTopologicalOrder (G : CDMG α) (r : α → α → Prop) : Prop where
  /-- `r` is irreflexive on the nodes of `G`. -/
  irrefl : ∀ v ∈ G, ¬ r v v
  /-- `r` is transitive on the nodes of `G`. -/
  trans : ∀ v ∈ G, ∀ w ∈ G, ∀ x ∈ G, r v w → r w x → r v x
  /-- `r` is trichotomous on the nodes of `G`: for any two nodes
  `v, w ∈ G`, at least one of `r v w`, `v = w`, `r w v` holds.
  Combined with `irrefl` and `trans`, "exactly one" is automatic
  (see the design-choice block above). -/
  trichotomous : ∀ v ∈ G, ∀ w ∈ G, r v w ∨ v = w ∨ r w v
  /-- Every parent precedes its children under `r`. The LN's
  `v ∈ \Pa^G(w) \implies v < w`. The `v ∈ G` / `w ∈ G` hypotheses
  the LN's "for all $v, w \in G$" prose suggests are automatic from
  `v ∈ Pa G w`: see `mem_Pa` (which gives `v ∈ G`) and
  `G.E_subset` (which gives `w ∈ G.V ⊆ G`). -/
  parent_lt : ∀ {v w : α}, v ∈ Pa G w → r v w

/-- `HasTopologicalOrder G` -- the existential statement "*there is*
a topological order of `G`". The right-hand side of the iff in
claim_3_2 (`lecture-notes/lecture_notes/graphs.tex`, Lem after
def_3_8): "$G$ is acyclic iff $G$ has a topological order". -/
def HasTopologicalOrder (G : CDMG α) : Prop :=
  ∃ r : α → α → Prop, IsTopologicalOrder G r
lean_code_without_comments:
structure IsTopologicalOrder (G : CDMG α) (r : α → α → Prop) : Prop where

  irrefl : ∀ v ∈ G, ¬ r v v

  trans : ∀ v ∈ G, ∀ w ∈ G, ∀ x ∈ G, r v w → r w x → r v x

  trichotomous : ∀ v ∈ G, ∀ w ∈ G, r v w ∨ v = w ∨ r w v

  parent_lt : ∀ {v w : α}, v ∈ Pa G w → r v w

def HasTopologicalOrder (G : CDMG α) : Prop :=
  ∃ r : α → α → Prop, IsTopologicalOrder G r
lean_explanation:
The variable `α : Type*` is the ambient vertex type — the same carrier the CDMG `G` (def 3.1) is built over. Lean wants every relation to specify its domain, so the candidate order `r : α → α → Prop` is named `r` rather than `<`: the symbol `<` is reserved for typeclass-resolved orders, and pushing the topological order through `LT` would force the whole carrier `α` to acquire a global order. The relation accepts arbitrary `α`-vertices, but the structure only constrains it on nodes of `G`.

The structure `IsTopologicalOrder G r` bundles four `Prop`-valued fields, in one-to-one correspondence with the LN's strict-total-order axioms plus the parent-respecting clause:

- `irrefl : ∀ v ∈ G, ¬ r v v` — the shorthand `v ∈ G` is membership in `G.J ∪ G.V`, so irreflexivity is asserted only on nodes of `G`.
- `trans : ∀ v ∈ G, ∀ w ∈ G, ∀ x ∈ G, r v w → r w x → r v x` — the three-fold version of the same `v ∈ G` localisation.
- `trichotomous : ∀ v ∈ G, ∀ w ∈ G, r v w ∨ v = w ∨ r w v` — the standard Mathlib shape, matching `Std.Trichotomous`. The LN's stronger "exactly one" reading follows from `irrefl` and `trans` (see the design notes).
- `parent_lt : ∀ {v w : α}, v ∈ Pa G w → r v w` — the LN's defining clause $v \in \mathrm{Pa}^G(w) \implies v < w$. The `{v w}` *strict-implicit* binders are a Lean idiom: the caller never spells out `v` or `w`, since Lean infers both from the hypothesis `h : v ∈ Pa G w`. Both `v ∈ G` and `w ∈ G` are already implicit consequences of `v ∈ Pa G w` (via the def_3_5 lemma `mem_Pa` and the CDMG axiom `G.E_subset`), so no extra membership hypotheses are needed.

`HasTopologicalOrder G` is the one-line existential closure `∃ r : α → α → Prop, IsTopologicalOrder G r`, packaging the LN prose *"$G$ has a topological order"*. It is the right-hand side of claim_3_2's iff (acyclic $\iff$ has a topological order) and the input shape every downstream chapter — do-calculus, the ID-algorithm, the iSCM solution theory — expects when it writes "let `G` be a CADMG with a fixed topological order".
design_choices:
**Relation on `α`, not a `LinearOrder` on a subtype.** The LN writes $v < w$ for bare vertices $v, w \in J \cup V$, and downstream chapters carry that habit forward (the ID-algorithm chapter writes things like $v_1 < v_2 < v_3$ throughout). The natural-looking alternative — a `LinearOrder` on the subtype `↥(G.J ∪ G.V)` — would force every such inequality to first inject `v` and `w` into the subtype before they could be compared, an ergonomic tax with no mathematical content. Carrying the relation as `α → α → Prop` and restricting the axioms to nodes of `G` is the cheapest encoding that supports both formal reasoning and the LN's notational habits.

**Axioms restricted to `v ∈ G`, not blanket over `α`.** The LN orders $J \cup V$, not the ambient carrier. A blanket strict total order on all of `α` would be strictly stronger — it would force trichotomy of vertices that don't appear in `G` and would prevent extending a topological order arbitrarily outside `G.J ∪ G.V`. The restricted shape is also natural for downstream reuse: claim_3_3 reuses `G`'s topological order on the intervened graph $G_{\mathrm{do}(W)}$, whose node set is the same but whose external behaviour is irrelevant.

**`structure ... : Prop` with named fields, not a four-way conjunction.** Four conjuncts is the point where named projections start paying off: downstream proofs reach for `hr.parent_lt`, `hr.trans`, `hr.irrefl` by name instead of peeling apart a four-deep `And.intro`. The structure form mirrors Mathlib's `Std.Asymm` / `Std.Trichotomous` / `IsTrans` style of single-field property classes and keeps the LN-aligned `parent_lt` name visible at every use site.

**Trichotomy stated as "at least one", not "exactly one".** The field reads `r v w ∨ v = w ∨ r w v`, matching `Std.Trichotomous`. The LN's "total order" reading is the exactly-one version, but together with `irrefl` and `trans` the stronger reading is automatic: `r v w ∧ v = w` substituted gives `r v v`, contradicting `irrefl`; `r v w ∧ r w v` with both endpoints in `G` transits to `r v v`, contradicting `irrefl` again. The lighter form loses no information.

**`parent_lt` takes `v ∈ Pa G w` directly, with no auxiliary `v ∈ G` / `w ∈ G` hypotheses.** Both memberships are implicit in `v ∈ Pa G w`: by `mem_Pa` it unfolds to `v ∈ G ∧ v ⟶[G] w`, and `G.E_subset : G.E ⊆ (J ∪ V) ×ˢ V` then forces `w ∈ G.V ⊆ G`. The trimmed form matches the LN literally and shaves a hypothesis off every caller.

**No `IsStrictTotalOrder` typeclass from Mathlib.** Mathlib's `IsStrictTotalOrder α r` is a strict total order on *all* of `α`, not on a subset. Forcing the encoding into it would require either fixing `α` to be a subtype (see the first bullet) or extending `r` to the whole carrier (extra structure not present in the LN). Spelling out the three strict-total-order axioms inline — restricted to nodes of `G` — is shorter and stays closer to the LN.

**`HasTopologicalOrder G` pulled out as its own `def`, not inlined as `∃ r, ...`.** Claim_3_2's iff and every later "let `G` be a CADMG with a fixed topological order" statement want a one-word spelling for the existential. Introducing `HasTopologicalOrder` gives both readings — relation-level `IsTopologicalOrder G r` and existential `HasTopologicalOrder G` — one canonical name each, and lets `simp` / `obtain` destructuring work against a single identifier. The relation-level form remains the right shape when the LN names the order (e.g. "let $<$ be a topological order" in the ID-algorithm chapter).

**No separate predicate for the LN's "indexing $v_1, \dots, v_K$" reading.** That description is the same notion restated in the finite case: the choice of a bijection `Fin K → α` whose image is `G.J ∪ G.V` and whose inherited `(· < ·)` agrees with the topological order. It is not formalised separately because (a) the LN treats the two descriptions as interchangeable rather than as distinct definitions; (b) doing so would commit the encoding to finiteness of `G.J ∪ G.V`, which the underlying CDMG type deliberately does not require (the vertex type `α` is fully polymorphic); and (c) downstream rows that genuinely need an indexing can derive one locally from `IsTopologicalOrder G r` together with a `Fintype` hypothesis, via Mathlib's `Fintype.equivFin`. Pre-emptively formalising it would bake in a redundancy.
